records.ety: [OF. trespasser to go across or over, transgress, F. trpasser to die; pref. tres- (L. trans across, over) + passer to pass. See Pass, v. i., and cf. Transpass.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Trespassed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Trespassing.]

defs: To pass beyond a limit or boundary; hence, to depart; to go.
quotes: Soon after this, noble Robert de Bruce . . . trespassed out of this uncertain world.

defs: To commit a trespass; esp., to enter unlawfully upon the land of another.
To go too far; to put any one to inconvenience by demand or importunity; to intrude; as, to trespass upon the time or patience of another.

To commit any offense, or to do any act that injures or annoys another; to violate any rule of rectitude, to the injury of another; hence, in a moral sense, to transgress voluntarily any divine law or command; to violate any known rule of duty; to sin; -- often followed by against.
quotes: In the time of his distress did he trespass yet more against the Lord.

records.ety: [OF. trespas, F. trpas death. See Trespass, v.]

defs: Any injury or offence done to another.
quotes: I you forgive all wholly this trespass.
If ye forgive not men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses.

defs: Any voluntary transgression of the moral law; any violation of a known rule of duty; sin.
quotes: The fatal trespass done by Eve.
You . . . who were dead in trespasses and sins.

defs: An unlawful act committed with force and violence (vi et armis) on the person, property, or relative rights of another.
An action for injuries accompanied with force.
